3:0.rendering: And the word of Samuel came to all Israel. Now Israel went out to meet the Philistines in battle, and they camped beside Ebenezer while the Philistines camped at Aphek.
3:0.note: Right after we hear that Samuel's prophetic word reaches the whole nation, Israel marches into battle against the Philistines at the border fortress of Aphek, near the stone of "Ebenezer" ("stone of help"). The stage is set for a story that will painfully echo the book of Judges: the people treat the ark like a lucky charm instead of seeking the God whose glory it represents, showing how quickly covenant memory can fade even when God's word is present.

3:1.rendering: The Philistines drew up their battle lines to meet Israel, and when the battle spread, Israel was defeated by the Philistines. About four thousand men from the ranks of Israel fell on the field that day.
3:1.note: This moment of crushing defeat echoes the larger biblical pattern of Israel’s unfaithfulness leading to exile-like loss, yet the story is just beginning. The ark of the covenant, God’s throne on earth, will soon be captured, forcing Israel (and the reader) to confront where true power and presence really reside, a thread that runs all the way to Jesus as the true temple and deliverer.

3:2.rendering: When the people came back to the camp, the elders of Israel said, "Why did Yahweh strike us today before the Philistines? Let us bring the ark of the covenant of Yahweh from Shiloh to us, so that it may come among us and save us from the hand of our enemies."
3:2.note: The elders treat the ark like a powerful talisman that can guarantee victory, echoing how the ancient Near East viewed divine images as carriers of a god's presence and power. This moment ironically foreshadows the Bible's bigger story about misplaced trust in religious objects instead of covenant faithfulness, a tension that will echo through the prophets and find its resolution when God's true presence comes to dwell among humanity in Jesus.

3:3.rendering: So the people sent to Shiloh and brought from there the ark of the covenant of the LORD of hosts, who is seated upon the cherubim. The two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were there with the ark of the covenant of God.
3:3.note: The title "LORD of hosts, who sits upon the cherubim" echoes the language of the tabernacle and temple, where heaven and earth overlap at God's throne, yet here the ark is treated like a magic battle talisman by a corrupt priesthood. This moment of misplaced trust in religious objects instead of covenant faithfulness sets the stage for the loss of God's presence, a pattern that will find its surprising resolution when the true King arrives and the glory of God tabernacles among us in Jesus.

3:4.rendering: When the ark of the covenant of the Lord came into the camp, all Israel let out a loud shout so that the ground shook.
3:4.note: The word for "shout" here is the same one used when Israel first met God at Sinai and when they later celebrated the ark entering Jerusalem under David, revealing how the Israelites treat the ark like a divine-warrior talisman, expecting heaven-and-earth overlap on their own terms. This moment in the unified story ironically sets up the Philistines capturing the ark, showing that God's presence cannot be controlled even as the narrative threads forward toward Jesus, the true temple where heaven and earth finally meet.

3:5.rendering: When the Philistines heard the sound of the shout, they said, "What is this loud shout in the camp of the Hebrews?" Then they learned that the ark of Yahweh had come into the camp.
3:5.note: The word for "shout" here is the same Hebrew term used for the thunderous celebration when the ark was brought into battle or procession, echoing its earlier role in the exodus and wilderness stories, but now ironically misused as a magical talisman. This moment in the larger narrative shows Israel's covenant unfaithfulness leading to the ark's capture, setting the stage for Yahweh's surprising victory over the Philistine gods and the eventual return of his presence, a thread that finds its ultimate echo in Jesus as the true temple presence.

3:6.rendering: The Philistines were afraid, because they said, "God has come into their camp." They said, "Woe to us! For nothing like this has ever happened before."
3:6.note: The word for "God" here is elohim, the same term the Philistines use for their own gods, creating an ironic twist as they sense Yahweh's terrifying presence among the Israelites, much like the dread that filled Egypt during the plagues. This moment echoes the larger biblical pattern of heaven and earth overlapping in unexpected ways, foreshadowing how God's presence would one day dwell among humanity in Jesus.

3:7.rendering: Woe to us! Who will rescue us from the hand of these mighty gods? These are the gods who struck the Egyptians with every kind of plague in the wilderness.
3:7.note: The terrified Philistines recognize Israel's God as the one who struck Egypt with plagues, but they call him "gods" (elohim) in the plural, missing that he is the singular creator who defeats every power. This moment echoes the exodus story while ironically setting up the capture of the ark, showing how even the nations sense the unified biblical theme of God's power over empires, a thread that runs from Egypt to Babylon and finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus' victory over the powers.

3:8.rendering: Strengthen yourselves and become men, you Philistines, or you will serve the Hebrews just as they have served you. Become men and fight.
3:8.note: This taunt from the Philistine commanders echoes the language of courage in battle found throughout the ancient world, yet it sets up one of the Bible's great ironies: the very next day Israel suffers crushing defeat, the ark is captured, and the Philistines temporarily "rule" over God's people. It subtly reminds us how the story of Israel is heading toward the ultimate king who would fight not with human strength but through apparent weakness and death.

3:9.rendering: So the Philistines fought, and Israel was defeated. Every man fled to his tent, and there was a very great slaughter, thirty thousand foot soldiers of Israel fell.
3:9.note: This scene of crushing defeat and the ark's capture echoes the curses of Deuteronomy 28, where covenant unfaithfulness leads to military disaster and exile from the land. Yet the story keeps pointing forward: the same God who lets his throne be taken will one day raise a greater King who defeats the ultimate enemy and restores his scattered people.

3:10.rendering: The ark of God was captured, and the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, died.
3:10.note: This verse is the dark climax of a story that began in 1 Samuel 1, where barren Hannah poured out her heart at the tabernacle and received a son she dedicated to God. Now her son Samuel has grown, but the very place she trusted has become corrupt, the priests who should represent heaven and earth have fallen in battle, and the symbol of God's presence among Israel has been taken by the Philistines, an echo of the garden exile where humanity's failure to guard sacred space leads to loss and scattering.

3:11.rendering: A man from Benjamin ran from the battle line and came to Shiloh that same day, with his clothes torn and dirt on his head.
3:11.note: The torn clothes and dirt on the head echo the classic ancient Near Eastern gestures of mourning, but they also quietly replay the imagery of Saul, the Benjaminite from an earlier part of the story, whose own rise began with signs of distress and torn garments. This lone survivor’s arrival sets the stage for the shocking report that the ark has been captured, a moment when heaven-and-earth overlap seems to collapse into exile, yet the larger narrative is already pointing toward God raising up a faithful priestly line and, ultimately, the anointed king who will bring the presence back.

3:12.rendering: When he arrived, Eli was sitting on his seat beside the road, watching, because his heart trembled for the ark of God. As the man came into the city and reported the news, the whole city cried out.
3:12.note: The Hebrew word for "trembled" here is the same one used when the Philistines trembled in fear before the ark in chapter 4, creating a subtle wordplay that shows even faithful Eli now shakes with dread. This moment sits at the hinge of Israel's story, where the ark's capture echoes the earlier loss of God's presence in the tabernacle at Shiloh, a pattern of exile from the divine presence that will find its ultimate resolution when Jesus, the true temple, brings heaven and earth back together.

3:13.rendering: Eli heard the sound of the outcry and said, "What is this uproar I am hearing?" The man hurried and came and reported it to Eli.
3:13.note: The Hebrew word for "uproar" (qol hamehumah) echoes the chaotic "sound" of the people's shout when the ark arrived in camp just verses earlier, creating a heartbreaking reversal: what began as a victory cry ends in disaster. This moment in the story of the ark's capture and Eli's death quietly prepares the ground for the return of God's presence, a pattern of exile and restoration that runs all the way to Jesus, the true temple who brings heaven and earth back together.

3:14.rendering: Now Eli was ninety-eight years old. His eyes were set, and he could not see.
3:14.note: The Hebrew word for "set" here literally means "stood" or "fixed," echoing the same rare phrasing used for Eli's failing eyes back in 1 Samuel 3:2, creating a bookend that frames the tragic section of his story. This detail paints Eli as an aging priest whose physical blindness mirrors the spiritual dimness in Israel during the time of the judges, a low point in the unified narrative that sets the stage for God raising up a new faithful leader in Samuel.

3:15.rendering: The man said to Eli, "I am the one who came from the battle line. I fled from the battle line today." And Eli said, "What was the matter, my son?"
3:15.note: The Hebrew word for "matter" here is dabar, the same term used for God's powerful word throughout Samuel, creating a poignant irony: Eli, whose ears had grown dull to the dabar of the Lord, now strains to hear a human dabar about disaster. This scene echoes the earlier divine word of judgment against Eli's house and weaves into the larger biblical pattern of exile and loss of God's presence, as the ark is captured and the glory departs from Israel.

3:16.rendering: The one who brought the news answered and said, "Israel has fled before the Philistines, and also a great defeat has come upon the people. Moreover, your two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, are dead, and the ark of God has been captured."
3:16.note: This devastating report forms the dark climax of a story that began with the deaths of Eli's corrupt sons (1 Samuel 2-3), echoing the earlier loss of the ark to enemies in the days of the judges. The word "captured" (lakach) for the ark creates a haunting irony, since the very symbol of God's presence among his people is now in Philistine hands, setting the stage for God's surprising return to fight for his own name in chapter 5 and ultimately pointing forward to the true King who would never lose the presence of God.

3:17.rendering: As soon as he mentioned the ark of God, Eli fell backward off his seat by the side of the gate. His neck broke, and he died, for he was an old man, heavy, and he had judged Israel for forty years.
3:17.note: The word "heavy" (kabed) here echoes its earlier use for Eli's worthless sons, who "made light of" (literally, treated as light) God's honor by despising the offerings (1 Samuel 2:17, 29-30). Now the glory (kabod) has departed from Israel (4:21-22), and the heavy priest who failed to guard sacred space dies at the news. This scene closes the era of the judges with the shocking loss of the ark, setting the stage for Yahweh himself to fight for his own name and eventually raise up a king after his own heart.

3:18.rendering: Now his daughter-in-law, the wife of Phinehas, was pregnant and about to give birth. When she heard the report that the ark of God had been captured and that her father-in-law and her husband were dead, she went into labor and gave birth, but her labor pains overwhelmed her.
3:18.note: The name of this woman is never given, yet her story forms a tragic echo of the larger narrative: the glory of God has departed from Israel, just as the name she gives her son, Ichabod, declares, "the glory has gone into exile." This moment weaves together themes of covenant unfaithfulness, the departure of God's presence, and the hope that the story is not yet finished.

3:19.rendering: As she was about to die, the women attending her said to her, "Don't be afraid, for you have given birth to a son." But she did not answer or pay attention. She named the boy Ichabod, saying, "The glory has gone into exile from Israel," because the ark of God had been captured and because of her father-in-law and her husband.
3:19.note: The Hebrew word for "glory" here (kabod) carries the weight of God's heavy, radiant presence that once filled the tabernacle, now shockingly "exiled" (a form of the same verb used for Israel's later deportation to Babylon). This scene forms a tragic hinge in the unified story: the loss of the ark signals the end of an era of corrupt leadership, yet it sets the stage for God raising up Samuel and eventually David, through whom the divine glory will one day return in a more permanent way, culminating in Jesus as the true image of God's glory among us.

3:20.rendering: She named the boy Ichabod, saying, "The glory has gone into exile from Israel," because the ark of God had been captured and because of her father-in-law and her husband.
3:20.note: The name "Ichabod" plays on the Hebrew phrase "where is the glory?" or "no glory," and the word for "gone into exile" (galah) is the same one used throughout the prophets for Israel's deportation to Babylon. This tragic birth scene at the end of the Eli story echoes the garden narrative's loss of God's presence and previews the greater exile to come, yet it sets the stage for the surprising return of the ark and, ultimately, the arrival of the true King who will restore God's glory to his people.

3:21.rendering: She said, "The glory has gone into exile from Israel," for the ark of God had been captured.
3:21.note: The word "glory" (kabod) carries the weight of God's heavy, manifest presence, and its "exile" (galah) here echoes the later Babylonian exile of Judah, showing how the loss of the ark signals the departure of heaven's presence from the land. This tragic moment in the unified story sets the stage for the true King who will restore God's glory among his people, a thread that runs from the tabernacle to the temple and ultimately to Jesus, the Word who tabernacles among us.

0:0.rendering: There was a certain man from Ramathaim of the Zuphites, from the hill country of Ephraim, whose name was Elkanah son of Jeroham son of Elihu son of Tohu son of Zuph, an Ephraimite.
0:0.note: This opening echoes the classic Hebrew storytelling formula for a hero's tale, yet the hero turns out to be a barren woman, not the man whose pedigree fills the verse.

0:1.rendering: He had two wives: the name of the one was Hannah, and the name of the other was Peninnah. Peninnah had children, but Hannah had no children.
0:1.note: The Hebrew wordplay between the wives' names, 'favor' and 'pearl,' already hints at the rivalry that will drive this family story.

0:2.rendering: This man used to go up from his city year by year to worship and to sacrifice to the LORD of hosts at Shiloh, where the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were priests of the LORD.
0:2.note: The title "LORD of hosts" first appears here, evoking the divine warrior who commands heavenly armies, right in the midst of a barren family's quiet desperation.

0:3.rendering: On the day when Elkanah offered the sacrifice, he would give portions to his wife Peninnah and to all her sons and daughters.
0:3.note: This verse echoes the ancient pattern of family meals after sacrifice, highlighting the painful contrast in a household divided by favoritism.

0:4.rendering: But to Hannah he would give a double portion, for he loved her, though the Lord had closed her womb.
0:4.note: The Hebrew literally says he gave her "one portion for two faces," an idiom that hints at special favor amid her barrenness.

0:5.rendering: Her rival also vexed her severely to irritate her, because the Lord had closed her womb.
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for "rival" (tsarah) carries the double meaning of "adversary," turning a domestic heartache into a battlefield of shame.

0:6.rendering: So it went year after year. Whenever she went up to the house of the Lord, her rival would provoke her, and she would weep and refuse to eat.
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "provoke" carries the sense of thunderous irritation, echoing the storm of emotion that will later birth Israel's prophet.

0:7.rendering: Her husband Elkanah said to her, "Hannah, why do you weep? Why do you not eat? Why is your heart bitter? Am I not better to you than ten sons?"
0:7.note: The repeated "why" questions echo the desperate cries of barren women throughout Genesis, yet Elkanah's tender but tone-deaf comfort highlights the communal ache no human love can fully mend.

0:8.rendering: After they had eaten and drunk in Shiloh, Hannah got up. The priest Eli was sitting on his seat by the doorpost of the temple of the LORD.
0:8.note: The Hebrew word for "got up" (qamah) echoes the same verb used when the barren woman "rises" to become a mother in other stories, hinting at the turning point in her long wait.

0:9.rendering: She was deeply bitter in soul, so she prayed to the LORD and wept bitterly.
0:9.note: The Hebrew repeats the root for "bitter" (marah) to show her soul's anguish matching her tears, an ancient way of signaling total emotional collapse before God.

0:10.rendering: And she vowed a vow and said, "Yahweh of hosts, if you will indeed look upon the affliction of your servant and remember me and not forget your servant, but will give to your servant a male child, then I will give him to Yahweh all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head."
0:10.note: Hannah's vow echoes the Nazirite laws of Numbers 6, binding her hoped-for son to lifelong holy service before he's even conceived.

0:11.rendering: As she kept on praying before the Lord, Eli was watching her mouth.
0:11.note: The Hebrew lingers on her lips moving silently, a detail that sets up Eli's mistaken judgment in the very next verse.

0:12.rendering: Now Hannah was speaking in her heart, only her lips moved but her voice was not heard, so Eli thought she was drunk.
0:12.note: In a culture where prayer was usually loud and public, Hannah's silent lips reveal desperate intimacy with God.

0:13.rendering: Eli said to her, "How long will you be drunk? Put away your wine."
0:13.note: The Hebrew word for "drunk" (shakar) also means "make oneself a fool," turning Eli's rebuke into an ironic echo of Hannah's earlier vow to dedicate her son away from wine.

0:14.rendering: But Hannah answered, "No, my lord. I am a woman hard of spirit. I have not been drinking wine or strong drink, but I have been pouring out my soul before the LORD."
0:14.note: The Hebrew "qasht-ruach" (hard of spirit) flips the expected insult into a picture of raw, poured-out honesty before God.

0:15.rendering: Do not consider your servant a worthless woman, for out of the abundance of my complaint and my vexation I have been speaking until now.
0:15.note: The Hebrew word for "worthless woman" echoes the same term used for the corrupt sons of Eli just verses earlier, turning Hannah's plea into a quiet contrast of true devotion against empty religion.

0:16.rendering: Then Eli answered her, "Go in peace. May the God of Israel grant the request you have asked of him."
0:16.note: Eli's words echo the priestly blessing of Numbers 6, shifting from suspicion to hopeful mediation in the ancient sanctuary.

0:17.rendering: She said, "May your servant find favor in your eyes." Then the woman went on her way, and she ate, and her face was no longer sad.
0:17.note: In the Hebrew, Hannah's face "was no longer" the same word used for her rival's taunting "provocation" earlier, showing her inner storm had been stilled.

0:18.rendering: They rose early in the morning, bowed down before the LORD, and returned to their house at Ramah. Elkanah knew his wife Hannah, and the LORD remembered her.
0:18.note: The Hebrew word for "remembered" here echoes God's remembering Noah in the flood and the Israelites in Egypt, signaling that this barren woman's story is about to turn.

0:19.rendering: And in the course of days Hannah conceived and bore a son. She called his name Samuel, for she said, "I asked him from the LORD."
0:19.note: The name Samuel echoes the Hebrew for "asked of God" or "heard by God," turning her desperate prayer into a living reminder of divine hearing.

0:20.rendering: Then the man Elkanah went up with his whole household to offer the yearly sacrifice to the Lord and to fulfill his vow.
0:20.note: This echoes the very vow Hannah just made, showing how her desperate prayer reshapes the entire family's rhythm of worship.

0:21.rendering: But Hannah did not go up with them, for she said to her husband, "I will not go up until the boy is weaned. Then I will bring him, so that he may appear before the Lord and stay there forever."
0:21.note: The Hebrew word for "weaned" carries the sense of being fully prepared, echoing how Samuel will one day be presented as a dedicated gift rather than a temporary loan.

0:22.rendering: And Elkanah her husband said to her, "Do what seems good to you. Stay until you have weaned him. Only may the Lord establish his word." So the woman stayed and nursed her son until she weaned him.
0:22.note: Elkanah's phrase "may the Lord establish his word" echoes God's earlier promise through the priest, quietly linking a mother's vow to the divine plan for Israel's future deliverer.

0:23.rendering: When she had weaned him, she took him up with her, along with a three-year-old bull, one ephah of flour, and a skin of wine, and she brought him to the house of the Lord at Shiloh. The child was still very young.
0:23.note: The Hebrew literally says she took him up with a three-year-old bull, echoing the dedication language of Exodus 13 where firstborn sons and animals both belong to the Lord.

0:24.rendering: Then they slaughtered the bull, and they brought the boy to Eli.
0:24.note: The boy's presentation echoes the dedication of Samuel as a lifelong Nazirite gift to Yahweh, a living sacrifice in place of the animal.

0:25.rendering: And she said, "Oh my lord, as your soul lives, my lord, I am the woman who stood here beside you, praying to the LORD.
0:25.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "stood" (nitzavti) echoes Hannah's earlier posture of desperate prayer right here in the sanctuary, now transformed into grateful witness.

0:26.rendering: For this child I prayed, and the Lord has granted me my petition that I asked of him.
0:26.note: The Hebrew word for "asked" (sha'al) echoes back to the boy's name, Samuel, which means "God has heard" or "asked of God."

0:27.rendering: Therefore I have lent him to the Lord. As long as he lives, he is lent to the Lord. And he worshiped the Lord there.
0:27.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "lent" (sha'al) echoes the name "Samuel" and foreshadows how this dedicated boy will become the one Israel "asks for" as prophet and kingmaker.

1:0.rendering: And Hannah prayed and said: My heart exults in the Lord, my horn is lifted high because of the Lord. My mouth is wide open against my enemies, for I rejoice in your salvation.
1:0.note: Hannah's song echoes the barren woman's triumph, her "horn lifted high" playing on the Hebrew word for strength that will later ring through David's victories and the Messiah's reign.

1:1.rendering: There is no one holy like the LORD, for there is no one besides you, and there is no rock like our God.
1:1.note: The Hebrew word for "rock" (tsur) echoes the wilderness stories where God provided water from the rock, painting him as the unshakeable source of life and refuge.

1:2.rendering: Talk no more with such lofty pride. Let no arrogance come from your mouth. For the Lord is a God who knows. And by him actions are weighed.
1:2.note: The Hebrew word for "knows" carries the sense of intimate weighing, echoing how God measured Cain's offering and will later test David's heart.

1:3.rendering: The bows of the mighty are shattered, but the feeble are girded with strength.
1:3.note: This reversal of fortunes echoes Hannah's own song, setting the pattern for how God topples the strong and lifts the weak throughout Samuel.

1:4.rendering: Those who were full have hired themselves out for bread, but those who were hungry have become full. The barren woman has borne seven children, but the one with many sons has grown feeble.
1:4.note: This reversal poem echoes Hannah's own story of infertility turned to fruitfulness, a pattern that runs through the whole Bible from Sarah to the church.

1:5.rendering: Yahweh kills and makes alive, he brings down to Sheol and brings up.
1:5.note: The Hebrew verb pair "kills and makes alive" echoes the ancient Near Eastern idea of a God who holds the keys to both death and life.

1:6.rendering: The LORD makes poor and makes rich, he brings low and he lifts up.
1:6.note: This verse echoes Hannah's own reversal from barrenness to motherhood, showing how the song's theology grows directly out of her lived experience.

1:7.rendering: He raises the poor from the dust, he lifts the needy from the ash heap, to seat them with princes, and he makes them inherit a throne of honor. For the pillars of the earth belong to the Lord, and on them he has set the world.
1:7.note: Hannah's song echoes the upside-down reversals that will mark God's kingdom, lifting the lowly while toppling the proud.

1:8.rendering: He guards the feet of his faithful ones, but the wicked are silenced in darkness, for not by strength does a man prevail.
1:8.note: The rare Hebrew verb for "guards" echoes the same word used for God's protective care of Israel in the wilderness, turning Hannah's personal victory into a picture of covenant loyalty.

1:9.rendering: Yahweh, let your adversaries be shattered. May he thunder against them from the heavens. Yahweh will judge the ends of the earth. He will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed one.
1:9.note: The final line introduces the first clear mention of "his anointed" in the Bible, a quiet seed for the messianic hope that will bloom across the story.

1:10.rendering: Then Elkanah went back to his house in Ramah, while the boy stayed behind to serve the LORD in the presence of Eli the priest.
1:10.note: This quiet handover marks the moment a barren woman's vow reshapes a family's future and begins training a prophet in the very sanctuary that had failed to hear her prayer.

1:11.rendering: Now the sons of Eli were worthless men. They did not know the Lord.
1:11.note: The Hebrew calls them "sons of Belial," a phrase that paints them as chaotic destroyers who treat God's covenant like a tool for personal gain.

1:12.rendering: Now this was the custom of the priests with the people: whenever anyone offered a sacrifice, the priest's servant would come while the meat was boiling, with a three-pronged fork in his hand.
1:12.note: The three-pronged fork echoes the repeated priestly "custom" wordplay in Hebrew, exposing how Eli's sons turned sacred ritual into personal plunder.

1:13.rendering: He would thrust it into the pan or kettle or cauldron or pot. Everything the fork brought up the priest would take for himself. This is how they treated all the Israelites who came to sacrifice at Shiloh.
1:13.note: The fork's greedy plunge into the boiling pot paints a vivid picture of ritual robbery, turning sacred offerings into a family feast.

1:14.rendering: Even before they burned the fat, the priest's servant would come and say to the man who was sacrificing, "Give meat for the priest to roast. He will not accept boiled meat from you, only raw."
1:14.note: The Hebrew highlights the brazen timing, snatching the raw flesh before the fat was even offered to God, exposing a system that treated holy offerings as personal property.

1:15.rendering: If the man said to him, "They will burn the fat first, then take as much as you want," he would say, "No, give it to me now. If not, I will take it by force."
1:15.note: The priest's servant overrides the law's careful sequence of burning fat to God before taking meat, exposing how greed twists sacred order into raw entitlement.

1:16.rendering: The sin of the young men was very great in the LORD's sight, for the men treated the offering of the LORD with contempt.
1:16.note: The word for "treated with contempt" echoes how Eli's sons "despised" God's law, showing their priestly corruption was not just sloppy but a direct rejection of Yahweh himself.

1:17.rendering: Samuel was ministering before the LORD, a boy wearing a linen ephod.
1:17.note: The linen ephod echoes the priestly garments of Aaron's line, quietly signaling that this boy from an ordinary family is already stepping into sacred space.

1:18.rendering: Each year his mother would make him a little robe and bring it to him when she went up with her husband to offer the yearly sacrifice.
1:18.note: The Hebrew word for this "little robe" echoes the priestly ephod, hinting that young Samuel is already being clothed for sacred service.

1:19.rendering: Eli would bless Elkanah and his wife, and say, "May the Lord give you children by this woman in place of the one she asked for from the Lord." Then they would go back to their own home.
1:19.note: Eli's blessing echoes Hannah's original vow, creating a quiet circle of gift and return in the family that will shape Israel's future.

1:20.rendering: And the LORD visited Hannah, and she conceived and bore three sons and two daughters. Meanwhile the boy Samuel grew up in the presence of the LORD.
1:20.note: The same verb for "visited" that God used to open barren wombs in Genesis now echoes again, showing how Hannah's gift back to God multiplies blessing in her own arms.

1:21.rendering: Now Eli was very old, and he heard all that his sons were doing to all Israel, and how they were sleeping with the women who served at the entrance of the tent of meeting.
1:21.note: The phrase "women who served" echoes the dedicated women at the tabernacle door in Exodus 38:8, turning a place of worship into a site of exploitation.

1:22.rendering: He said to them, "Why do you do such things? For I hear of your evil dealings from all this people."
1:22.note: The father's repeated question echoes the earlier divine warning in 1 Samuel 2:29 about honoring sons above God.

1:23.rendering: No, my sons. The report I hear is not good. You are making the Lord's people transgress.
1:23.note: The Hebrew word for "report" (shemu'ah) echoes the opening of 1 Samuel, where the same term described the devastating news about Eli's priestly house, now circling back as his own sons create the scandal.

1:24.rendering: If a man sins against another man, God will mediate for him, but if a man sins against the LORD, who will intercede for him? But they would not listen to the voice of their father, for it was the LORD's will to put them to death.
1:24.note: The Hebrew word for "mediate" echoes the priestly role of standing between, yet here even Eli's intercession fails against willful sin against God himself.

1:25.rendering: And the boy Samuel kept on growing in stature and in favor both with the LORD and with the people.
1:25.note: This echoes the growth pattern of young Joseph in Genesis, hinting at God's hand on unlikely leaders in Israel's story.

1:26.rendering: A man of God came to Eli and said to him, "This is what the Lord says: 'I clearly revealed myself to your father's family when they were in Egypt under Pharaoh's house."
1:26.note: The phrase "revealed myself" literally means "I was revealed, revealed," an emphatic Hebrew repetition that underscores God's dramatic self-disclosure to the Levitical line.

1:27.rendering: I chose your father's house out of all the tribes of Israel to be my priests, to go up to my altar, to burn incense, to wear the linen ephod before me. I also gave your father's house all the offerings made by fire from the Israelites.
1:27.note: This echoes the ancient covenant with Aaron's line in Exodus and Leviticus, yet the very election that once honored Levi's family now sets the stage for its judgment.

1:28.rendering: Why do you scorn my sacrifice and my offering that I commanded for my dwelling place? Why do you honor your sons more than me by making yourselves fat from the best parts of every offering of my people Israel?
1:28.note: The Hebrew word for "honor" here is the same root used for "glorify" in verse 30, exposing the bitter irony of Eli's misplaced loyalty.

1:29.rendering: Therefore the LORD, the God of Israel, declares: I said that your house and the house of your father would walk before me forever, but now the LORD declares: Far be it from me! For those who honor me I will honor, and those who despise me will be lightly esteemed.
1:29.note: God reverses his own earlier promise to Eli's family, showing that divine commitment and human honor form a two-way street in ancient covenant thinking.

1:30.rendering: Look, the days are coming when I will cut off your arm and the arm of your father's house, so that there will not be an old man in your house.
1:30.note: This oracle against Eli's priestly line uses the Hebrew idiom "cut off your arm" for the violent removal of strength and descendants, echoing the curse on the house of Eli that began in chapter 1.

1:31.rendering: Then you will see distress in the place where God makes his dwelling, and all the good that is done for Israel, and there will never be an old man in your house.
1:31.note: This oracle plays on the Hebrew word for "dwell" (ma'on), echoing the very name of the shrine at Shiloh where Eli's family served, turning their privileged place into the site of their coming grief.

1:32.rendering: Yet I will not cut off every man of yours from my altar, but your eyes will fail and your soul will grieve. All the increase of your house will die by the sword of men.
1:32.note: This oracle plays on the Hebrew word for "cut off" (karat), ironically sparing one survivor while the rest of Eli's house is severed from the priesthood.

1:33.rendering: This will be the sign for you that will come on your two sons, Hophni and Phinehas: both of them will die on the same day.
1:33.note: This grim sign to Eli echoes the earlier judgment on his house, tying priestly failure to the rise of a faithful new leader.

1:34.rendering: I will raise up for myself a faithful priest. He will do according to what is in my heart and in my soul. I will build him a sure house, and he will walk before my anointed one all the days.
1:34.note: The phrase "raise up for myself a faithful priest" echoes God's promise to David in 2 Samuel 7, linking priestly and royal lines in one enduring house.

1:35.rendering: Then everyone who is left in your house will come and bow down to him for a piece of silver or a loaf of bread, and will say, Please assign me to one of the priests' offices so that I may eat a piece of bread.
1:35.note: The once-proud priestly house now begs for scraps and secondary jobs, a bitter reversal echoing God's earlier warning that the faithful would be honored while the arrogant would be cut off.

2:0.rendering: The boy Samuel was serving the LORD under Eli. In those days the word of the LORD was rare, and there was no widespread vision.
2:0.note: The Hebrew word for "rare" also means "precious," hinting that God's silence made every scrap of revelation feel like treasure in a famine.

2:1.rendering: Now Eli's eyes had begun to grow dim, he could not see well, and at that time Samuel was lying down in the temple of the LORD where the ark of God was.
2:1.note: Eli's failing eyesight foreshadows both his physical and spiritual blindness as Israel's high priest.

2:2.rendering: The lamp of God had not yet gone out in the temple of the LORD where the ark of God was, and Samuel was lying down in the temple of the LORD.
2:2.note: The flickering lamp echoes the tabernacle's perpetual light in Exodus 27:20, signaling that even in a time of spiritual dimness God's presence still burns low but not out.

2:3.rendering: Then the LORD called to Samuel, and he said, "Here I am."
2:3.note: This "Here I am" echoes Abraham, Moses, and Isaiah's ready responses to God's call, signaling Samuel's unexpected readiness as a boy in a time when divine words were rare.

2:4.rendering: He ran to Eli and said, "Here I am, for you called me." But Eli said, "I did not call. Go back and lie down." So he went and lay down.
2:4.note: The boy's eager sprint to Eli echoes Abraham's ready "Here I am" at God's call, yet here the voice is still mysterious.

2:5.rendering: Yahweh called again, "Samuel!" And Samuel got up, went to Eli, and said, "Here I am, for you called me." But Eli said, "I did not call, my son. Go back and lie down."
2:5.note: The Hebrew verb for "called" repeats like a drumbeat, echoing God's persistent voice in a time when prophetic words were rare.

2:6.rendering: Now Samuel did not yet know the Lord, and the word of the Lord had not yet been revealed to him.
2:6.note: The Hebrew repeats "did not yet know" and "had not yet been revealed," underscoring how even a boy sleeping in the sanctuary needed God's own initiative to truly encounter him.

2:7.rendering: Yahweh called Samuel a third time. He got up, went to Eli, and said, "Here I am, for you called me." Then Eli understood that Yahweh was calling the boy.
2:7.note: The Hebrew word for "called" echoes the very name of the boy Samuel, meaning "God has heard" or "called by God."

2:8.rendering: So Eli said to Samuel, "Go lie down, and if someone calls to you, say, 'Speak, LORD, for your servant is listening.'" Then Samuel went and lay down in his place.
2:8.note: The Hebrew word for "listening" (shomea) echoes the opening command of the Shema in Deuteronomy 6:4, turning a sleepy boy into the next link in Israel's chain of obedient hearers.

2:9.rendering: Then the LORD came and stood there, and called as at the other times, "Samuel, Samuel!" And Samuel said, "Speak, for your servant is listening."
2:9.note: The double "Samuel, Samuel!" echoes God's urgent calls to Abraham, Jacob, and Moses, signaling a pivotal moment of revelation and commissioning.

2:10.rendering: Then the LORD said to Samuel: Look, I am about to do something in Israel that will make both ears of everyone who hears it ring.
2:10.note: The Hebrew verb for "ring" (tsalal) echoes the earlier description of Eli's failing eyes, creating a paired image of sensory overload: what the old priest can barely see, the whole nation will be unable to un-hear.

2:11.rendering: On that day I will carry out against Eli everything I have spoken concerning his house, from beginning to end.
2:11.note: The Hebrew idiom "from beginning to end" echoes God's earlier word to Eli in 2:27-36, showing divine patience has run its measured course.

2:12.rendering: I told him that I would judge his house forever for the iniquity that he knew about, because his sons were cursing God and he did not restrain them.
2:12.note: The Hebrew word for "cursing" here is actually "blessing" used in a sarcastic or euphemistic sense, a common ancient Near Eastern way to avoid directly naming blasphemy.

2:13.rendering: Therefore I have sworn concerning the house of Eli: the guilt of his house will never be atoned for by sacrifice or offering, forever.
2:13.note: The word "sworn" echoes God's unbreakable oath to Abraham, now turned in judgment against a priestly family that refused to honor him.

2:14.rendering: Samuel got up early in the morning and opened the doors of the house of the Lord. He was afraid to tell Eli the vision.
2:14.note: The Hebrew word for "opened" echoes the earlier breaking of the doors in 1 Samuel 1:9, framing the young prophet's first reluctant dawn of obedience.

2:15.rendering: Then Eli called Samuel and said, "Samuel, my son." And he said, "Here I am."
2:15.note: The exact phrase "my son" and the obedient reply "Here I am" echo God's earlier call to Abraham in Genesis 22:1,11, quietly signaling that young Samuel now stands in the place of Israel's tested covenant heir.

2:16.rendering: And he said, "What is the word that he spoke to you? Do not hide it from me. May God do so to you and more if you hide from me a single word of all that he spoke to you."
2:16.note: Eli's oath formula, "may God do so to you and more," echoes ancient treaty curses and foreshadows the judgment soon to fall on his own house.

2:17.rendering: So Samuel told him everything, he hid nothing from him. And Eli said, "He is the LORD. Let him do what seems good to him."
2:17.note: Eli's resigned words echo the ancient practice of submitting to a sovereign decree, a posture later echoed in Jesus' garden prayer.

2:18.rendering: So Samuel grew, and Yahweh was with him, and he let none of his words fall to the ground.
2:18.note: The phrase "let none of his words fall to the ground" echoes the ancient idea of a reliable prophet whose oracles never drop uselessly like failed arrows or seed, a motif that will later mark true messengers from Moses to Jesus.

2:19.rendering: All Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew that Samuel was confirmed as a prophet of the LORD.
2:19.note: From Dan to Beersheba marks the entire land of Israel, a phrase that echoes the nation's full extent and signals Samuel's role as the unifying bridge between judges and kings.

2:20.rendering: And the LORD continued to appear at Shiloh, for the LORD revealed himself to Samuel at Shiloh by the word of the LORD.
2:20.note: The rare phrase "revealed himself by the word" echoes God's pattern of self-disclosure first seen with Abraham, now resuming after centuries of rare visions in Israel.
